item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's odd, this seems to be a compelling, touching story about someone learning to forgive themselves after a traumatic accident that's been disguised as a generic "shoot the weird thing, amnestitize everyone else" SCP. Don't get me wrong, it's a fantastic piece of writing, but it's a shitty SCP- I mean, there's no explanation of why it's so dangerous as to be Keter class, or how it spreads.

Also, the effects are such overt metaphors for the symptoms of depression (e.g., looking/feeling grey, giving up, fading away) that I have to come to the conclusion that you must have started with a great psychological tale that you then haphazardly adapted into a cliché-riddled SCP. I'm pretty sure that if this were just a story set in the real world, of sessions held between a normal psychologist and a man with a normal mental disorder, it would have been better. I'm not sure it would've been able to be posted on this site, but that's not a bad thing. It just means it's not related. Lots of people here have written amazing pieces of fiction that will never see the light of day on this site because they have nothing to do with it. If you have something amazing to write, then write it, just don't compromise it for the sake of making it in-universe so you can post it to this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love both the idea and the execution of this SCP and some details hold it back from reaching its full potential.
In my mind, two types of stories exist: ones where if you really think about it some details don't make sense and stories where this process occurs during the story. If the latter happens it can detract from a strong story and in this case the minor details just jumped at me. Don't get me wrong, it reads like a good story, good dialogue, good characters! However, immediately after (and partly during) reading I had questions which did not go answered:

  • Why did nobody look into the background of Elijah? Surely the twist ending is something even a superficial background check should have uncovered!
  • Subjects "in the later stages" should be immediately terminated. Why, exactly? If the therapy log proves one thing it is that this is far from being the point of no return. And what does constitute a "later stage" anyways? When the subject sees everything as dim? When there is a distortion in the eyes? On part of the body? Or when the whole body is covered?
  • Why may MTF ETA-62 "White Noise" only consist of people with no background in mental illness? This together with the instruction to terminate people implies the effect is contagious and nothing in the article indicates that it is.
+ Suggestion
- Hide Suggestion

I think that points 2 and 3 could be addressed by stating something like "While a significant amount of subjects affected by SCP-3234 have perceived an instance of SCP-3234 before developing symptoms themselves, roughly █% of instances did not." I'm not good with the clinical tone, but this would at least establish *why* exactly termination is important and *why* the MTF needs to consist of people not susceptible to SCP-3234. By keeping the option of spontaneous open it would also remain Keter.

As it stands it seems like the SCP was contagious at some point in development and when this was changed, not all aspects have been adjusted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is this sort of an anomalous Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder? Or rather, an anomalous coping mechanism with traumatic memories? If so, then I am impressed. The ultimate hiding from self, so to speak. The treatment logs made this, without them this would be bland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a true scp. It feels so human, like this actually happened the story telling was beautiful. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't find this terribly interesting and ducked out when I realized how long the interviews were getting, but I wanted to ask: are the typos intentional? Like the "SCO" designation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This brought me back to my late teens (17-19-year-old me would have needed to see this), but I was pleasantly surprised by the happy ending. Those are rare on this site. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, this is… really dark. You deserve a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice to see this skip on the site! good job and good writing! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: thank you so much! :) I'm glad it felt real to'ya

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this SCP, the dialog really felt real and this nearly brought me to tears, holy crap…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was expecting this to turn out to be some sort of memetic predator like in SCP-3002, but this ending works better. Maybe I'm reading too much into this, but it seems like people trying to will themselves out of existence after a traumatic event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was unexpectedly painful. I'm going to have to come back and read it through again, which I find I often do with particularly moving SCPs. The subject seemed authentically human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to everyone in site19 for all the crit on this. Hopefully it lives a nice long life.
